reason: Methods describe a scikit-learn "Random forest regression" model with no mention anywhere in the text of permutation, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance; the Ext. Data Fig. 9b/c legends explicitly state "the y-axis shows the feature importance as calculated by the predictive model," i.e. RandomForestRegressor's default impurity/MDI importance. This ranking underlies specific stated conclusions: "BCL2L1 expression was the most important feature in predicting MCL1 dependency," "the top predictive feature for MDM2 dependency was RNA expression of EDA2R... as a surrogate marker for functional p53," and "Predictive feature modeling for HDAC2 highlighted RNA expression of its known target FUCA1. This suggests that lower expression of FUCA1 indicates pediatric cell lines with high HDAC activity." The only corroboration offered is (a) bivariate scatter/linear-fit plots of the single top feature versus dependency score (Fig. 3c; Ext. Data Fig. 8b) and (b) overall model accuracy via 5-fold CV Pearson correlation between predicted and observed CERES scores. Neither validates that the reported feature actually outranks other correlated candidates in the multivariate importance computation: per the stated rubric, overall model accuracy does not validate the selection, and a bivariate plot of only the winning feature cannot demonstrate it beat competing features in a fair (bias-corrected) comparison. No permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional-importance analysis appears anywhere in the full text, methods, or supplementary table descriptions (Supplementary Table 6 just reports "feature importance" per top feature from the same RF models). The MCL1 functional follow-up (CRISPR knockout, S63845 inhibitor sensitivity) validates that the MCL1 dependency itself is real, not that BCL2L1 specifically (versus other correlated BCL-2-family transcripts or confounders) is the true top predictor. All three CONFIRM criteria are therefore met: genuine impurity importance use, centrality to stated conclusions, and lack of adequate corroboration.
